svg.title: How DBSCAN identifies clusters and noise
svg.desc: A three-step diagram explaining DBSCAN. First, choose a neighborhood radius epsilon and a minimum number of points MinPts, including the point itself. Second, classify points as core, border, or noise. The illustrated geometry is internally consistent with epsilon 76 diagram units and MinPts 5. Third, core points connected by chains of epsilon-neighbor relationships form clusters, and non-core points within epsilon of a core point may join as border points. Shaded cluster regions are illustrative.

text.tspan: DBSCAN finds dense, connected groups of points without requiring
the number of clusters in advance.
Set a neighborhood radius ε and a
minimum number of points, MinPts.
Here, |Nε(p)| = 6 ≥ 5,
so the focal point is core.
MinPts includes the point itself.
Neighborhood density determines whether
a point is core, border, or noise.
Core: at least MinPts points within ε
Border: not core, but within ε of a core
Noise: not within ε of any core point
Chains of ε-neighboring core points
form clusters; nearby border points join.
Irregularly shaped clusters are possible.
A border point touching two clusters may
join whichever cluster reaches it first.
Core point: |Nε(p)| ≥ MinPts
Nε(p) includes points no farther than ε from p, including p.
Scale features before computing distances.
Smaller ε or larger MinPts is stricter and usually
creates more noise; the reverse may merge groups.
